speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is March the 17th, 1997. My name is Linda Davidson, L-I-N-D-A D-A-V-I-D-S-O-N. I'm interviewing the survivor Dr. Hanna Strawczynski, nee Rychter. H-A-N-N-A, last name S-T-R-A-W-C-Z-Y-N-S-K-I, maiden name R-Y-C-H-T-E-R. The interview is being conducted in English and taking place in Montreal, Quebec, Canada. Today's date is March the 17th, 1997. My name is Linda Davidson, L-I-N-D-A D-A-V-I-D-S-O-N. I'm interviewing the survivor Dr. Hanna Strawczynski, born Rychter, H-A-N-N-A, last name S-T-R-A-W-C-Z-Y-N-S-K-I, maiden name R-Y-C-H-T-E-R. The interview is being conducted in English and taking place in Montreal, Quebec, Canada. Please introduce yourself, your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Hanna Strawczynski. My maiden name is Rychter. I was born in Poland, Czestochowa, which is a city in the southwestern part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 17th of October, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does everybody call you Hanna?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it is not usual, actually, in Poland to be called by a full, official name. My name is Hanka, H-A-N-K-A, and this is the way I am known to my friends and my family. I didn't use it much here in Canada. Here I am Hanna, but it is not the Hanna with an H at the end, it's a different name. It's close to Anna or Anne. It's the same name, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you were living in Poland, your last name, Strawczynski, would that be a little different, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it would be a little different. It would be Strawczynska, and actually this is what it is-- the '-ski' at the end signifies a male, and it is '-a' at the end for females. However, here, for legal reasons, we were told that it's better to have the same, exact spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czestochowa, do you-- can you tell us your pre-war address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I lived on Aleja Wolnosci, which means the boulevard of freedom. A very nice street. Number 19. I remember it well and I saw it after the war several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us? The name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aleja, A-L-E-J-A. Wolnosci, W-O-L-N-O-S-C-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell for us Czestochowa as well, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. C-Z-E-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. We'd like to start off learning about your family. If you could tell us about your mom, her name, where she was born, and what kind of family, how long they had lived in the area, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Both my mother and my father were born in Czestochowa. They were both born in 1987, my mother in February and my father in June, 1987.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: June--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1897. Did I make a mistake?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1897. My father's family was a fairly large traditional Jewish family. Orthodox. I remember my mother wearing a wig, a [HEBREW]. I didn't know my grandfather-- he-- on my father's side. He died before I was born. My grandfather worked in a sawmill. He was a worker. This was a very poor family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Lajb. And they had six sons. My father was, I believe, the second oldest. My father-- my father's name was David-- David. David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a nickname that people used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [POLISH], maybe. It is sort of a diminutive. He was loved by people. He had every sunny disposition and he was a very kind man, so everybody liked him very much. And he was not very tall. He was rather on the small side, so they used to call him [POLISH]. My father as a child went to cheder, and then for a few years he was in Yeshiva, but I was told, you know, like, family tales-- my mother used to tell me a lot about it-- he was not a very good student there. Instead of being a very good student he drew pictures, so he didn't last long there. He was a very talented man. He drew, he painted, and he was a writer, but I'll talk about it maybe a little bit later. As a very young boy at the age of 15, my father started to work also in a factory in Czestochowa, and this is, I think, where he was exposed to the working class, both Jewish and Polish, and where he started to be involved and interested in the plight of the poor. He, at the age of 18, became a member of the [POLISH], SDKPL, which was the Social Democracy of the Kingdom of Poland and Lithuania. It was the party of Rosa Luxembourg and the precursor of the Polish Communist Party.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did his family respond to this affiliation? How did his family, his parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a very interesting question. How did this family of traditional Jewish people respond to this affiliation? I only know that he was always a member of the family. They loved him, they protected him. They took my mother in, who also had similar political views. So they must have been very tolerant people and very loving people. He was not the only one to be a communist in this family because the oldest son, who emigrated to Uruguay before the war and I never knew him, was also a communist.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Maurice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what occupation did your father pursue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father became very involved in the work of the-- of his party. He became a union organizer and publicist, and he was writing for different left-wing, of course, papers, both Yiddish and Polish. He did all types of things during his life, but this was the main thing. The Communist Party was illegal in Poland, so he was not a legal person. Therefore, he did not live long with his family in Czestochowa because this, being a rather small town, they-- the police knew him very well, and he did get arrested many times. And he was also prison terms several times.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, he became a-- this became an illegal existence, and he lived in Warsaw. There were many communists in this generation-- many Jewish communists. They-- they were doubly persecuted, I think. They felt it very strongly and doubly as Jews. The persistent antisemitism, the racial thing, and also the poverty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is why the Polish Communist Party had a large number of Jews. It doesn't mean that all Jews were communists-- this was a minority, I'm sure. But the party itself had a lot of-- of Jewish communists.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to hear about your mom-- her name and what kind of person she was.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother's story was even more unusual, maybe, than my father's story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name first.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came-- her name was Felicia-- Fella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klein. She was born to a-- also a very poor family of 10. 10 children-- family of 12. She started working as a young girl. She didn't have much education. She had maybe three, four years of school and started working at a young age at a factory of dolls. It was celluloid dolls. She, at work, met a young man-- not a Jew, a young, Christian Pole, and it was a great love of, but, of course, there was no way for her family to accept a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: So around the age of-- I believe she must have been 18 or 19, just before the end of the second-- the first World War, she left her family, and she lived with this young man. The family said shiva, as if she died. And I never knew my mother's family. She never had any contact with them anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the very end of the first World War in 1918, the young man was in the street and he was killed by a stray bullet. Of course I never knew this story before the war. I mean, when mother was alive. I only learned it after the war when I met a former friend and the neighbor of my mother's family. And she, Mrs. [POLISH]. Mrs. [POLISH] was-- knew a lot about my mother, and she told me this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings? Brother, sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother who was 4 and 1/2 years older than I was. His name was Heinrich-- [POLISH]. He was born in 1924. So the-- the three of us-- I mean, as far as I can remember, my-- my father did not live with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Warsaw, and we would join him as a family only during school vacations. So every summer vacation, all three of us would go to Warsaw. And sometimes when there was money, they would rent a little place in the country close to Warsaw. And this is when we were together. Occasionally we would go to see my father also during spring break or these type of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the effect on the family of your father living so far away, and financially and otherwise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well financially, the situation was very bad. We never had money. My father's earning from his writing, from his other work, were very scanty and very irregular. My mother was an incredibly brave and energetic woman, and the burden of running the family, keeping us clothed and-- and fed, and bringing us up was on her shoulder. She did all types of things. She-- before she had children, she was working in a hospital. I'm not exactly-- I don't know exactly what capacity. Probably helped to nurse. So she learned how to make injections and other little medical tasks. She would do it privately. Later on, she learned how to do a manicure, and she would have her clients coming to our room. I say 'room' because we lived in one room. It was a large room, a front room of a nice building in the nice part of the city. However, this room did not have water and did not have a bathroom. We had nice furniture-- a little, not very much. But the furniture was designed by my father and made-to-order, so I remember this wardrobe that was a combination of bookshelves and wardrobe. Now it is more common to see this type of thing, but then it was a novelty. And there was a daybed, and I, with my mother, slept in the daybed. And then on the other side of the window, there was a little sofa, and this is when my brother slept. And there was also a small desk where we did our-- our homework, and a table where we ate. And in addition, there was this very narrow table where my mother worked on her manicure between the two beds, just under the window. In the back of the room, there was a-- a stove, a coal stove, and a pail of clean water and a pail of dirty water behind a curtain. And it was very, very clean, and nobody coming in would ever imagine that behind the curtain there is, you know, this type of thing. To the bathroom we had to go to the second courtyard, and I was very much afraid to go there as a child. I never went alone. During the night, of course, if necessary, we used a chamber pot. I remember close to this latrine in the second courtyard there lived a family-- very large. There must have been 14 children. Family of a Jewish cobbler. Incredibly poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember their name. Well, maybe I do-- [POLISH]. I'm not sure. And I always was able to appreciate that comparing to them, we were very rich. We were always very nicely dressed and-- and fed properly, but my mother was performing some miracles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your happiest memories from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: My happiest memories. My happiest memories were, of course, being together with my father, summer vacation. These were very, very happy times. There were always people, friends, of my parents coming in. There were these great political discussions that I was listening to. Some fights between the Bund and the communists and the Zionists, and we were always fascinated by that. My mother took, also, a very vivid part in all this. She didn't have much education, but she was a very intelligent woman. She-- she loved music. So did my father. And we always, always were exposed to classical music, to opera music.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak among yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Yeah. However, my-- my parents did speak Yiddish occasionally among themselves. And my father was-- he was-- he was a real polyglot, and every language he spoke was absolutely beautiful. He-- but he was called-- he was a [YIDDISH], and I don't know how to translate it into English, but it means that she really loved Jewish culture, the Yiddish language, and he wrote a lot in Yiddish. He also wrote in Polish, and-- and in his Polish writings, I remember the verses, the poems, that he would send us. And some of them were sent-- he once had a long term in jail. I was only 3 then. And we were receiving from jail these beautiful little poems in Polish for children, and I still remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us one from memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I could. It will take maybe two minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- my mother sent him a picture of the two children just holding hands together, and he sent back this beautiful piece of paper with a drawing of this picture, only on this drawing, we wore masks. And the little poem was called [POLISH]. It means 'Who are you?' And it goes like that. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the verse, and basically what it means is he speculates who we could be, who we are. Are we rich? Or are we poor? Are we, maybe, some terrible bandits, like Alibaba and others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe we are little rebels who would like to build a new, good life all over the world. And he promises that he will never tell anybody, so please tell. It's-- my husband used a very sophisticated language in everyday language that he spoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't, obviously, understand the words, but anyone could hear the rhythm and the music and the rhyming--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, there is a rhyming, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --throughout.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a good rhyming.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very discernible. Thank you. What I wanted to-- to ask you-- what kind of schooling you had in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa, we first went, both my brother and I, to a Polish government school. How do you say it? However, when I was in grade 2, there was a search in our house, a night search. They were looking for illegal literature. They were maybe looking for-- for my father. I don't remember. And after that, we were told that we cannot attend these public schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think it's as a result of his communist--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a result of the communism, yeah. So finally, my mother sent us to a private school that was an expensive school, but she-- either she paid very little or she didn't pay at all. And this was called the Jewish gymnasium. It was a very good school in Czestochowa. It was a Polish-language school. All the instructions were in Polish except for the Bible and-- and Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for three years, I stayed in this school until the war in grade 3, 4-- grade-- no, maybe I was in grade 3 one year, so it was 4, 5, and 6 I spent in the school. And you mentioned that it was a good school. What are the activities that you remember in particular from the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember little ballet classes where we danced classical ballet. I enjoyed it very much. There was a wonderful choir. There was a wonderful orchestra. My brother was playing the trumpet and he was very, very musical. He also whistled beautifully. And I remember school performances-- very, very exciting. All types of very interesting things. It was a happy school. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that stage, did you lose contact with your non-Jewish friends from the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mixed quite freely--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too-- I was too young then. We just made a transition. I don't remember anybody from grade 1 and grade 2, no.
